result.question: How do existing systems implement in-process / embedded hooks and triggers where a database or GraphQL engine written in Rust/C/C++ calls user code in a host language runtime (especially Go)? Context: we are building a Hasura-v2-compatible GraphQL engine in Rust and want to embed it into a Go application as a library (cgo), exposing pre_insert / post_insert (sync, in-transaction) and post-commit hooks as native Go functions instead of webhooks. Research questions: (1) Real-world precedents of Rust/C engines embedded in Go with reverse callbacks (Go functions called from the engine) — e.g. SQLite hooks in mattn/go-sqlite3 or zombiezen, DuckDB UDFs in go-duckdb, libSQL/Turso, RocksDB compaction filters/merge operators in grocksdb, V8/wasm runtimes (wasmtime-go, wazero host functions), CockroachDB/Pebble, Dgraph/Badger patterns, NATS embedded mode. How do they design the FFI boundary: callbacks vs pull/poll APIs, batching, payload serialization (JSON vs binary), memory ownership, panic/recover across boundary, goroutine/thread attachment, shutdown/drain semantics? (2) How do GraphQL/backend engines expose lifecycle hooks natively: Hasura event triggers/actions (webhook model), Supabase, PostGraphile plugins (in-process JS), Pocketbase (in-process Go hooks + JSVM), Encore, Ent ORM hooks, gqlgen middleware — what semantics do they pick for pre/post mutation hooks (can modify rows? abort transaction? run after commit? at-most-once vs at-least-once)? (3) Known performance data and pitfalls of cgo reverse callbacks at high call rates (cgo call overhead numbers in modern Go, thread attachment cost, GOMAXPROCS vs foreign thread pools, known production war stories e.g. from grocksdb, go-sqlite3, wasmtime-go). (4) What alternatives do people actually choose in production and why: webhooks/sidecar, embedded wasm (extism, wazero), embedded JS (goja, v8go), CDC/logical replication consumers, in-process journal + callback hybrid. Deliverable: a cited report mapping the design space, what the most common/battle-tested pattern is for each sub-problem, and concrete recommendations applicable to our Rust-engine-in-Go design (pull-based event consumer + sync batched hooks via a single exported trampoline).
result.summary: Battle-tested Go bindings for native engines (mattn/go-sqlite3, grocksdb) converge on a single pattern for reverse callbacks: a thread-safe Go-side registry of callbacks addressed by an opaque handle (malloc'd token or integer index), passed through the engine as user-data and resolved in a small fixed set of //export-ed trampoline functions — never raw Go function pointers, which the cgo pointer rules make illegal. Since Go 1.17, runtime/cgo.Handle is the stdlib-sanctioned version of this exact round-trip pattern, and the cgo rules dictate the memory-ownership contract: the Rust side must copy or C-allocate any payload it retains, and Go hook results must be non-pointer data, C buffers, or pinned memory. There is solid precedent for synchronous, abortable, row-mutating in-process hooks (SQLite UDF errors abort the statement in-flight; RocksDB compaction filters delete/rewrite values; PocketBase's e.Next() interceptor chain lets pre-hooks modify or abort before the INSERT), but callbacks may arrive concurrently from foreign engine threads with thread-safety left entirely to the Go author. Performance evidence strongly supports the proposed design of batching across the boundary: per-call cgo overhead is modest (~171ns in 2015, ~40ns in modern Go), but CockroachDB's production data shows the real killer is scheduler interaction — a goroutine in a cgo call holds its P for up to ~20µs before sysmon releases it, collapsing throughput ~6.5x under 100 concurrent callers, and moving batching to the Go side of the boundary doubled performance; the Go team formally declined a fast 'nonblocking' cgo path in 2016, so this overhead is structural and must be amortized, not eliminated. Net recommendation: the contemplated design (pull-based event consumer for post-commit events + synchronous batched in-transaction hooks dispatched through a single exported trampoline with cgo.Handle-style registry) matches the most common battle-tested pattern on every sub-problem.

findings.claim: The dominant, battle-tested FFI design for engine-calls-Go callbacks is a handle registry plus fixed exported trampolines: mattn/go-sqlite3 uses a mutex-protected map keyed by a 1-byte C.malloc'd opaque pointer and one //export trampoline per hook category (UDF, aggregate, collation, commit/rollback, update, authorizer, pre-update), each resolving the Go closure from SQLite's user-data; grocksdb uses a thread-safe copy-on-write list (COWList) of Go filter objects addressed by integer index, with //export-ed gorocksdb_compactionfilter_* trampolines receiving the index from C. In neither library does C ever hold a Go pointer.
findings.evidence: Verified verbatim against both primary repos (fetched 2026-06-12): go-sqlite3's newHandle() does C.malloc(C.size_t(1)) and stores the callback in handleVals keyed by that pointer; all trampolines (callbackTrampoline, commitHookTrampoline, updateHookTrampoline, preUpdateHookTrampoline, etc.) resolve via lookupHandle(). grocksdb's registerCompactionFilter appends to COWList and SetCompactionFilter passes the integer as C.uintptr_t state; gorocksdb_compactionfilter_filter resolves via compactionFilters.Get(idx).

findings.claim: runtime/cgo.Handle (Go 1.17, CL 295369, golang/go#37033, authored by Changkun Ou) is the stdlib-sanctioned generalization of this pattern: an opaque uintptr integer representing any Go value, with the documented intended use being exactly the round-trip needed here — Go creates a handle with NewHandle, passes it as C uintptr_t into the engine, the engine passes it back through an //export-ed trampoline, and Go resolves it via Handle.Value() plus type assertion. The official cmd/cgo docs name it as the safe mechanism for passing Go values through C.
findings.evidence: Verified against the live Go 1.25.5 toolchain: go doc runtime/cgo.NewHandle contains verbatim 'The intended use is to pass the returned handle to C code, which passes it back to Go, which calls Value', and the package example implements the single-exported-trampoline dispatch. Go 1.17 release notes and the merged commit confirm authorship and CL. Caveat from verifiers: Handle is sync.Map-backed (~487–674 ns/op in the CL's own benchmark), so extremely hot paths sometimes use custom registries like grocksdb's COWList.

findings.claim: The cgo pointer-passing rules make handle indirection effectively mandatory and dictate the memory-ownership contract for an embedded Rust engine: (a) passing a Go pointer containing Go pointers (channels, closures, structs-of-pointers) to C panics at runtime ('cgo argument has Go pointer to unpinned Go pointer'); (b) C/Rust may not retain a Go pointer after the call returns unless pinned with runtime.Pinner, and strings/slices/channels cannot be pinned at all — so row payloads crossing the boundary must be copied or C-allocated if retained; (c) a Go trampoline called from C may only return/store non-pointer data, C pointers, or Go pointers to pinned memory, constraining hook result passing.
findings.evidence: All rules verified verbatim in Go 1.25.5's cmd/cgo/doc.go (lines 386–413) and reproduced empirically by a verifier (channel-pointer panic on Go 1.25.5, including with attempted Pinner). One nuance: long-lived pinning of a slice's backing array does allow Rust to retain a pointer to payload bytes across calls if the Go side keeps the Pinner alive — but slice/string/channel header values are never pinnable, so copy-or-C-allocate remains the default rule.
findings.vote: 3-0, 3-0, 2-1, 3-0 (claims 7, 11, 12, 13 merged; the 2-1 dissent concerned only the Pinner exception nuance)

findings.claim: There is real-world precedent for every hook semantic the dist-api design needs. Sync + abortable: go-sqlite3's callbackError() converts a Go error to sqlite3_result_error, failing the SQL statement in-flight (and SQLite's commit hook aborts via nonzero return, the authorizer via SQLITE_DENY). Row-mutating: grocksdb's Filter(level, key, val) (remove bool, newVal []byte) lets the embedded Go hook delete keys or rewrite values in-process. Pre/post ordering with explicit continuation: PocketBase's in-process Go hooks use a middleware interceptor chain where handlers must call e.Next() to proceed — code before e.Next() runs before validation and the INSERT (can modify e.Record or abort by returning an error), and OnRecordAfterCreateSuccess fires only after commit.
findings.evidence: All verified against primary sources: callbackError() body and its call sites in functionInfo.Call/aggInfo confirm synchronous abort (precision caveat: this path covers UDF/aggregate callbacks; SQLite's update/pre-update hooks are void and observe-only, while commit-hook/authorizer abort via separate return-value mechanisms). grocksdb's Filter signature and doc comments confirm delete/rewrite semantics. PocketBase docs verbatim: handlers 'expect the user to call e.Next() if they want to proceed with the execution chain'; GitHub discussions confirm pre-e.Next() errors abort/roll back.
findings.vote: 2-1, 3-0, 3-0 (claims 2, 5, 24 merged; the 2-1 was a scope-precision dissent, not a contradiction)

findings.claim: Reverse callbacks arrive on the engine's native (foreign) threads, synchronously and potentially concurrently, with thread-safety left entirely to the Go callback author: grocksdb documents that RocksDB's multithreaded compaction may call a shared Filter instance from different threads concurrently and 'the application must ensure that the call is thread-safe'; the binding adds no synchronization. For dist-api this means Go hook callbacks invoked from Rust tokio/worker threads must either be documented as concurrent-safe or be serialized by the engine before crossing the boundary.
findings.evidence: Quote verified verbatim in grocksdb compaction_filter.go and independently confirmed by RocksDB upstream compaction_filter.h ('CompactionFilter methods may be called from different threads concurrently... must ensure that such calls are thread-safe'). The C trampoline blocks the native compaction thread on the Go return value per key-value pair.

findings.claim: Per-call cgo overhead is roughly two orders of magnitude above a native Go call but small in absolute terms, and has improved over time: ~171ns vs 1.83ns Go call (CockroachDB benchmark, Go 1.5 era, 2015); 'about 170ns and up (600-800 cycles), ~100x a Go call' (Dmitry Vyukov's numbers relayed in golang/go#16051, 2016); ~60ns trivial call, ~35x a Go call (navytux, Go 1.10, 2018), with effective overhead roughly doubling under GOMAXPROCS>1 with other goroutines due to futex-mediated cross-thread churn; verifiers corroborate ~40ns in modern Go 1.21+. Conclusion for the design: a single boundary crossing per hook batch is cheap; per-row crossings at high rates are not.
findings.evidence: All three historical figures verified verbatim against primary sources (blog benchmark output, GitHub comments fetched via API). Verifiers independently cross-checked the modern ~40ns figure (Go 1.21 benchmarks). All numbers are explicitly date-stamped; only the ~40ns figure should be quoted as current.

findings.claim: The real high-call-rate hazard is Go scheduler interaction, not per-call ns cost: a goroutine in a cgo call keeps holding its GOMAXPROCS slot (P), and only after ~20µs does the sysmon thread treat it as blocked and release the P (Ian Lance Taylor, golang/go#19574; actual retake delay can reach 10-17ms due to sysmon's adaptive sleep) — so many concurrent medium-length C calls (exactly the profile of in-transaction hooks doing I/O) starve the scheduler unless GOMAXPROCS is raised or calls are funneled/batched. Additionally, each blocking cgo call pins an OS thread the runtime cannot multiplex, carrying a megabyte-scale C stack, and such threads are never reclaimed (golang/go#14592).
findings.evidence: Taylor's quote verified verbatim via GitHub API (2017-03-16 comment); mechanism confirmed still current in modern Go (runtime/cgocall.go still uses entersyscall; golang/go#57103 re-confirms _Psyscall/sysmon retake in Go 1.19; issue still open). Thread-stack claim verified in the CockroachDB post and corroborated by open runtime issues #14592 and #71150 (2025, cgo thread stacks staying in RSS). Caveat: the ~8MB pthread stack is lazily committed virtual memory initially.

findings.claim: CockroachDB's production war story is the strongest precedent for Go-side batching as the mitigation: with 100 concurrent workers calling RocksDB through cgo, throughput collapsed roughly 6.5x (8,200 ops/sec pure-Go vs degrading to ~1,245 ops/sec via cgo), and moving write-batch grouping from C++ into Go improved performance ~100% while reducing latencies. Their broader mitigation strategy was structural, not per-call: vendoring C deps into Go-buildable wrapper packages (c-rocksdb, c-snappy, c-protobuf, c-jemalloc, c-lz4) and moving whole operations across the boundary to reduce crossing frequency — ultimately culminating in replacing RocksDB with pure-Go Pebble.
findings.evidence: All figures verified against the raw issue body via GitHub API (8,029-8,432 ops/sec pure-Go table; exactly 1,245.5 ops/sec at the 4s mark; 100 worker default; PR #14138 'improved performance on one experiment by 100%'). Marked medium because the batching claim drew a 2-1 vote: the dissent correctly notes the diagnosed root cause was scheduler starvation under many concurrent blocking calls (per-call overhead was called 'negligible' in the issue itself), and raising GOMAXPROCS to 100 was an alternative workaround recovering ~7,000 ops/sec — batching is the adopted and proven mitigation, but not the only one, and the throughput collapse was unstable rather than flat.

findings.claim: cgo overhead is structural and will not be engineered away: the Go team formally declined the '#cgo nonblocking' fast-call proposal on 2016-10-24 ('Marking some cgo calls as non-blocking can fail in too many subtle ways'), instead speeding up the general path in Go 1.8 (CL 30080). Taylor's rationale: a goroutine in C is non-preemptible (blocking GC and scheduling), so scheduler notification cannot be safely skipped, and stack switching plus C ABI argument passing are unavoidable — a 'nonblocking' path would save little. Design implication: amortize the fixed cost via batching and a pull-based consumer rather than hoping for a faster FFI.
findings.evidence: Decline comment and rationale verified verbatim via GitHub API (ianlancetaylor, 2016-06-16 and 2016-10-24); CL 30080 confirmed merged 2016-09-30 in the 1.8 cycle via Gerrit API. Still current: Go has no nonblocking cgo path as of Go 1.25; async preemption (1.14+) does not preempt goroutines in C; unsafe community hacks (fastcgo/directcgo) achieve speedups precisely by accepting the unsafety Taylor described. Nuance: Go 1.22 added #cgo noescape/nocallback annotations, so the decline is not a permanent ban on all cgo annotations.

result.caveats: (1) Coverage gap: large parts of research questions 2 and 4 did not survive verification — no confirmed claims about Hasura event-trigger delivery semantics, PostGraphile/Supabase/Ent/gqlgen hook semantics, DuckDB/libSQL/wasmtime-go/wazero FFI designs, or the webhook/wasm/embedded-JS/CDC alternative comparison; only PocketBase represents the 'GraphQL/backend engine native hooks' category, so the at-most-once vs at-least-once and post-commit-durability design space is under-evidenced here. (2) Time-sensitivity of performance data: the headline cgo numbers (171ns/2015, 170-200ns/2016, 60ns/2018) are explicitly historical; only ~40ns (Go 1.21+, secondary-source corroboration) should be treated as current, and the CockroachDB 6.5x collapse predates Go 1.8 scheduler improvements — the qualitative mechanism (P-holding, sysmon retake) is confirmed current via still-open runtime issues, but the magnitude on modern Go is unmeasured. (3) Three merged claims carried 2-1 votes: the SQLite abort path applies to UDF/aggregate callbacks (SQLite's literal update hooks are observe-only; abort goes through commit-hook/authorizer return values); the CockroachDB batching story has a scheduler-starvation root cause where raising GOMAXPROCS was an alternative fix; the 'cannot retain Go pointers' rule has a long-lived-Pinner exception for backing arrays. (4) cgo.Handle's sync.Map-backed implementation (~0.5µs/op under contention) may itself need replacing with a grocksdb-style indexed registry on very hot hook paths — flagged by verifiers but not benchmarked in this research. (5) grocksdb compaction filters fire during background compaction, not at write time, so that precedent supports 'in-process hook can mutate data', not write-path latency characteristics.
openQuestions: What is the measured overhead and thread-attachment cost of the reverse direction specifically — foreign Rust/tokio threads calling into Go via //export (needm/dropm machinery for non-Go-created threads) — as opposed to the well-measured Go-to-C direction, and does pre-attaching a fixed pool of engine threads that call into Go amortize it?

What delivery semantics (at-most-once vs at-least-once, ordering, durability across restart) do production systems pick for post-commit hooks when moving from webhooks to in-process callbacks — e.g., does an in-process journal + pull-based consumer (the contemplated design) have a battle-tested precedent in libSQL/Turso embedded replicas, Badger value-log subscribers, or NATS JetStream embedded mode?

How do wasm-based alternatives (wazero host functions, extism) and embedded-JS engines (goja, v8go) compare in practice to cgo reverse callbacks for hook execution — both in per-call overhead and in operational safety (no foreign threads, no pointer rules) — and at what hook call rate does each become the better trade?

Do Go 1.22's #cgo noescape/nocallback annotations and runtime.Pinner-based zero-copy payload passing materially change the batching calculus for a new design in 2026, i.e., can per-row sync hooks be made cheap enough to skip Go-side batching for small transactions?
